data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: When simplifying using indices laws, always apply the power to every term inside brackets. For example, (2xy)² = 4x²y², not 2x²y². This is a top mark strategy.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Students often add exponents incorrectly: x² × x³ ≠ x⁵ (it equals x⁵, but many write x⁶). Also remember: x⁰ = 1 for all x ≠ 0, and x⁻ⁿ = 1/xⁿ, not 1/n.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Always simplify surds by factoring out perfect squares first. √72 = √(36 × 2) = 6√2. This is quicker and avoids leaving numbers in non-simplified form, which examiners penalise.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: √a + √b ≠ √(a+b). You cannot combine surds under addition unless they have the same radicand. For example, 2√3 + 3√3 = 5√3, but 2√3 + 3√2 cannot be simplified further.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: When one equation is linear, rearrange it first to express one variable in terms of the other, then substitute into the quadratic. This minimises algebra errors and makes solutions clearer.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Always check both solutions in the original equations—examiners expect this. A quadratic+linear system can yield 0, 1, or 2 solutions. If the discriminant is negative, there are no real solutions (tangency or no intersection).

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Use elimination when coefficients are already aligned or easily scaled. Multiply one or both equations by small integers to make one variable cancel cleanly. This method is elegant and reduces arithmetic errors.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: When eliminating, multiply the entire equation—not just one term. Sign errors when multiplying are extremely common. Always check: did I multiply both sides by the same value and preserve all terms?

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Substitution excels when one equation already has a variable isolated (e.g., y = 3x + 2 or x = 5). Rearrange first if needed, then substitute carefully to avoid errors. Show your working step-by-step.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: After substituting, solve completely for one variable before back-substituting. Many students forget to substitute back to find the second variable, leaving their answer incomplete.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Always reverse the inequality sign when multiplying or dividing by a negative number. This is essential and frequently tested. Colour-code negative operations in your working to avoid this critical error.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Treat inequalities like equations until the final step. Many students forget to reverse the inequality symbol entirely. Also, ensure your number line shows a clear open circle (< or >) or closed circle (≤ or ≥).

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Always sketch the parabola to verify your solution. Examiners call this 'sketching the curve'—it shows you understand the geometry. Mark roots and show whether the parabola is positive or negative between them.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Forgetting to include the roots themselves in the solution when using ≤ or ≥. Also, if the quadratic doesn't factorise, use the quadratic formula. Don't abandon the question without exploring all methods.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Test a point inside and outside each boundary line to determine which side of the line to shade. Use the origin (0, 0) unless it lies on the boundary. Shade the correct region clearly and label each line with its equation.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Shading the wrong region is a common error. Remember: y > x means above the line, but y < x means below. Use a test point to verify. Also, use solid lines for ≤ or ≥, and dashed lines for < or >.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Check the leading coefficient's sign first: positive means the parabola opens upward (for cubics, ends high on right), negative means downward. This guides your entire sketch and helps catch errors quickly.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Confusing repeated roots with simple roots. A double root means the curve touches the axis but doesn't cross (stationary point). A triple root means the curve crosses and has a horizontal tangent. Mark these distinctions clearly on your sketch.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Always state the form of proportion clearly: 'y ∝ xⁿ', then write 'y = kxⁿ' and solve for k using given values. This structured approach shows understanding and minimises algebraic errors.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Forgetting to find the constant of proportionality k. Many students state y = x² but stop there without substituting data to find the actual equation. Always complete the equation before solving for unknowns.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Remember: f(x) + a moves the graph UP by a units (vertical), and f(x - a) moves it RIGHT by a units (horizontal). Rewrite f(x + a) as f(x - (-a)) to see movement LEFT. Use vectors to describe and verify translations.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Confusing the sign in f(x + a). Students often think f(x + a) moves right, but it actually moves LEFT by a. Similarly, f(x) - a moves DOWN, not up. Sketch one point to verify your direction.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Test reflection by transforming a known point. For y = -f(x), reflect (a, b) to (a, -b). For y = f(-x), reflect (a, b) to (-a, b). Mark these transformed points clearly on your sketch to validate the reflection.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Forgetting which axis the reflection is across. y = -f(x) reflects in the X-axis (horizontal line). y = f(-x) reflects in the Y-axis (vertical line). Use mnemonics or examples to keep this clear.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: For conjugate denominators like a + √b, multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate a - √b. This creates a difference of squares: (a + √b)(a - √b) = a² - b, eliminating all surds from the denominator.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Forgetting to multiply the numerator by the conjugate. Many students multiply only the denominator, leaving surds in both places. Remember: multiply BOTH numerator and denominator by the SAME conjugate.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: For y = af(x), the scale factor is a (vertical stretch). Key points (x, y) become (x, ay). For y = f(ax), the scale factor is 1/a (horizontal stretch: wider if a < 1). Always transform coordinates to show understanding.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Confusing scale factors in y = f(ax). Students incorrectly think the horizontal stretch is by factor a, when it's actually 1/a. If a = 2, the graph compresses horizontally (narrower, scale 1/2). Test with a point to verify.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Graphical methods are lifelines when algebraic methods fail. Plot both curves carefully, identify intersection points, and use these coordinates as your solution. State approximate values to 1 d.p. and show reasoning clearly.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Sketching rough, inaccurate graphs that hide intersection points. Use a ruler, plot grid points carefully, and mark asymptotes. Also, don't forget to check that your graphical solution aligns with algebraic work.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: For reciprocal graphs, always identify the asymptotes: x = 0 (vertical) and y = 0 (horizontal). Mark these with dashed lines. Show clearly which quadrant(s) the curve occupies, and label key points like (1, k) on y = k/x.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Forgetting that y = k/x has two separate branches. If k > 0, one branch is in quadrants I and III. If k < 0, branches are in quadrants II and IV. Also, transformations move asymptotes: y = k/(x-a) has vertical asymptote at x = a.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Use FOIL systematically for double brackets: (a + b)(c + d) = ac + ad + bc + bd. Check your answer by expanding backwards or using the identities (a±b)² and (a+b)(a-b). This verification catches errors immediately.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Students often forget the middle term when expanding (a + b)². It's (a + b)² = a² + 2ab + b², not a² + b². Similarly, (a - b)² = a² - 2ab + b². The '2ab' term is critical.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Try factorising in this order: (1) common factors, (2) difference of squares, (3) trinomials, (4) grouping or the factor theorem. Always check your answer by expanding. For cubics, use the factor theorem to find one root first.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Forgetting to factorise completely. For example, 2x² - 8 = 2(x² - 4) = 2(x - 2)(x + 2). Stopping at 2(x² - 4) loses marks. Also, always check if a common factor exists before trying other methods.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Choose the solving method strategically: (1) Factorisation is fastest if roots are integers, (2) Completing the square shows form and insight, (3) Quadratic formula is reliable for any equation. State which method you're using.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: In the quadratic formula, the ± symbol means two separate values. Many students compute only one root or make sign errors. Also, remember the formula is x = (-b ± √(b² - 4ac)) / 2a, not / a.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: The discriminant Δ = b² - 4ac tells you everything: Δ > 0 means 2 distinct roots, Δ = 0 means 1 repeated root, Δ < 0 means no real roots. State the discriminant value explicitly in your solution.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Miscalculating b² - 4ac by ignoring signs. If b is negative, b² is still positive. Also, watch the coefficient a: 2x² - 3x + 1 has a = 2, b = -3, c = 1. Errors here lead to wrong discriminant values.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Completing the square reveals the vertex immediately. For y = (x - h)² + k, the vertex is (h, k) and axis of symmetry is x = h. This is quicker than using calculus and examiners love seeing completed-square form.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: When a ≠ 1, many students forget to divide through by a first. For 2x² + 8x + 5, write 2(x² + 4x) + 5, complete the square inside, then simplify. Also, don't lose track of brackets when moving constant terms.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Always define your substitution clearly: let u = x² or u = sin(x), etc. Solve for u first using the quadratic formula or factorisation, then substitute back to find all x values. Show both steps explicitly.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Forgetting to substitute back after solving for u. If u = 2, you're not done—you must solve x² = 2 (for example) to find x = ±√2. Also, some u-values may yield no real x-solutions; check carefully.

data.title: Examiner Tip
data.text: Always identify five key features: (1) direction (positive/negative leading coefficient), (2) y-intercept (c), (3) roots (set y = 0), (4) vertex (x = -b/2a), (5) behaviour. Showing all five demonstrates complete understanding.

data.title: Common Mistake
data.text: Sketching a parabola that doesn't pass through the y-intercept or misses the roots entirely. Always mark the y-intercept and label roots on the x-axis. Use a scale and grid lines so your sketch is readable and accurate.
